title: Definitions
content: This section contains comprehensive definitions used throughout the Act. Key terms relevant to startups include: 'assessee' means a person by whom any tax or any other sum of money is payable under this Act; 'assessment year' means the period of twelve months commencing on the 1st day of April every year; 'previous year' means the financial year immediately preceding the assessment year; 'income' includes profits and gains, dividend, voluntary contributions received by a trust, the value of any perquisite or profit in lieu of salary, any capital gains, and any sum chargeable to income tax; 'person' includes an individual, a Hindu undivided family, a company, a firm, an association of persons, a body of individuals, a local authority, and every artificial juridical person; 'total income' means the total amount of income computed in accordance with the provisions of this Act.

title: Charge of Income Tax
content: Where any Central Act enacts that income tax shall be charged for any assessment year at any rate or rates, income tax at that rate or those rates shall be charged for that year in accordance with, and subject to the provisions of, this Act in respect of the total income of the previous year of every person. Income tax is an annual tax levied on the total income of every person, and the liability to pay income tax arises by virtue of the charging section read together with the Finance Act of the relevant year which specifies the rates of tax. The total income of every person is computed in accordance with the provisions of this Act.

title: Income Deemed to Accrue or Arise in India
content: Income shall be deemed to accrue or arise in India if it accrues or arises through or from any business connection in India, or through or from any property in India, or through transfer of a capital asset situated in India. Salary income is deemed to accrue in India if it is earned in India. Income by way of interest, royalty, or fees for technical services payable by the Government, or by a person resident in India, is deemed to accrue in India. This section has significant implications for startups receiving foreign investment or engaging in cross-border transactions, as it determines the taxability of non-resident income in India.

title: Exemption - Sum Received Under Life Insurance Policy
content: Any sum received under a life insurance policy, including the sum allocated by way of bonus on such policy, is exempt from income tax. However, this exemption does not apply where the premium payable for any of the years during the term of the policy exceeds ten percent of the actual capital sum assured (fifteen percent for policies issued before April 1, 2012). For policies issued on or after April 1, 2023, where the aggregate premium exceeds five lakh rupees, the income from such policies shall not be exempt. The exemption continues to apply to sums received on the death of the insured person regardless of premium amount.

title: Exemption - Long-Term Capital Gains on Listed Equity (Repealed)
content: This section previously exempted long-term capital gains arising from the transfer of equity shares in a company or units of an equity-oriented fund or units of a business trust where the transaction was chargeable to Securities Transaction Tax (STT). This exemption was withdrawn by the Finance Act, 2018, with effect from April 1, 2018, and replaced by Section 112A which taxes such long-term capital gains exceeding one lakh rupees at the rate of ten percent without indexation benefit. This change was significant for startup founders and investors who had previously enjoyed tax-free exits on listed equity holdings.

title: Capital Gains
content: Any profits or gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset effected in the previous year shall be chargeable to income tax under the head 'Capital gains' and shall be deemed to be the income of the previous year in which the transfer took place. The capital gains shall be computed by deducting from the full value of the consideration received or accruing as a result of the transfer, the cost of acquisition of the asset and the cost of any improvement thereto, as adjusted for indexation in case of long-term capital assets. This section is particularly relevant for startups during equity transactions, mergers, acquisitions, and when founders or early investors sell their stake.

title: Income from Other Sources - Angel Tax
content: Where a company, not being a company in which the public are substantially interested, receives in any previous year from any person being a resident, any consideration for issue of shares that exceeds the face value of such shares, the aggregate consideration received for such shares as exceeds the fair market value of the shares shall be chargeable to income tax as income from other sources. This provision, commonly known as the 'Angel Tax,' was introduced to curb money laundering through inflated share premiums. Startups recognized by DPIIT under the Startup India initiative may claim exemption from this provision. The Finance Act, 2023, extended this provision to also cover consideration received from non-residents.

title: Deduction for Eligible Startups
content: Where the gross total income of an eligible startup includes any profits and gains derived from an eligible business, a deduction of an amount equal to one hundred percent of the profits and gains derived from such business shall be allowed for three consecutive assessment years out of ten years beginning from the year of incorporation. An eligible startup means a company or LLP incorporated between April 1, 2016, and March 31, 2025 (extended to March 31, 2025 by Finance Act, 2024), whose turnover does not exceed one hundred crore rupees in the relevant previous year, and which holds a certificate of eligible business from the Inter-Ministerial Board of Certification as notified by the Central Government. The startup must be engaged in innovation, development, or improvement of products, processes, or services, or must be a scalable business model with high potential of employment generation or wealth creation.

title: Tax on Income of New Manufacturing Domestic Companies
content: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, the income tax payable in respect of the total income of a person, being a domestic company, for any previous year relevant to the assessment year beginning on or after April 1, 2020, shall be computed at the rate of fifteen percent at the option of the company, subject to the condition that the total income has been computed without claiming any exemption or deduction under specified provisions, the company has been set up and registered on or after October 1, 2019, and has commenced manufacturing or production of an article or thing on or before March 31, 2024 (extended to March 31, 2024). The effective tax rate including surcharge and cess is approximately 17.16 percent. This provision was introduced to attract fresh investment in manufacturing and make India competitive with other manufacturing hubs.

title: Tax on Income of Certain Domestic Companies
content: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, the income tax payable in respect of the total income of a domestic company for any previous year relevant to the assessment year beginning on or after April 1, 2020, shall at the option of the company be computed at the rate of twenty-two percent, subject to the condition that the total income is computed without claiming any exemption or deduction under specified sections. The effective tax rate including surcharge and cess comes to approximately 25.17 percent. This concessional rate was introduced by the Taxation Laws (Amendment) Act, 2019, to make Indian corporate tax rates competitive globally. Startups that do not wish to claim deductions under Section 80-IAC may opt for this lower flat rate.

title: Return of Income
content: Every person whose total income or the total income of any other person in respect of which he is assessable exceeds the maximum amount which is not chargeable to income tax shall furnish a return of income on or before the due date. The due date for companies is October 31 of the assessment year, and for persons whose accounts are required to be audited, the same date applies. For other assessees, the due date is July 31. A belated return may be filed before three months prior to the end of the relevant assessment year or before the completion of assessment, whichever is earlier. Every company and firm must file a return of income irrespective of whether income exceeds the exemption limit or not.

title: Assessment
content: Where a return has been made under Section 139, or in response to a notice under Section 142(1), the return shall first be processed by way of intimation under Section 143(1) within nine months from the end of the financial year in which the return is furnished. In the intimation, the total income or loss shall be computed after making adjustments for arithmetical errors, incorrect claims, disallowances, and additions as apparent from the return. Thereafter, the Assessing Officer may serve a notice under Section 143(2) requiring the assessee to attend the office or produce evidence, and after hearing such evidence, the Assessing Officer shall pass an assessment order under Section 143(3) determining the total income.

title: Dividends
content: The principal officer of an Indian company or a company which has made the prescribed arrangements for the declaration and payment of dividends within India shall, before making any payment of any dividend or distributing or paying any dividend to any shareholder who is resident in India, deduct income tax thereon at the rate of ten percent. No deduction shall be made from any dividend referred to in Section 115-O (which was applicable till March 31, 2020). With effect from April 1, 2020, dividends are taxable in the hands of the recipient, and TDS is required to be deducted at ten percent where aggregate dividend to a resident exceeds five thousand rupees in a financial year.

title: TDS on E-commerce Transactions
content: Every e-commerce operator, being a person who owns, operates, or manages digital or electronic facility or platform for electronic commerce, shall at the time of credit of amount of sale or service to the account of an e-commerce participant or at the time of payment, whichever is earlier, deduct income tax at the rate of one percent of the gross amount of such sale or service. This provision, effective from October 1, 2020, is particularly relevant for startups operating as e-commerce platforms or selling through e-commerce marketplaces. An e-commerce participant who is an individual or HUF with gross sales not exceeding five lakh rupees and who has furnished a PAN or Aadhaar is exempt from this TDS.

title: TCS on Sale of Goods
content: Every person, being a seller, who receives any amount as consideration for sale of any goods of the value or aggregate of such value exceeding fifty lakh rupees in any previous year, shall at the time of receipt of such amount collect from the buyer a sum equal to 0.1 percent of the sale consideration exceeding fifty lakh rupees as income tax. This provision does not apply to goods on which TDS is deductible under any other provision of the Act or TCS is collectible under other sub-sections of Section 206C. The provision is relevant for startups involved in B2B sales of goods exceeding the threshold.

title: Set Off of Refunds Against Tax Remaining Payable
content: Where under any of the provisions of this Act a refund is found to be due to any person, the Assessing Officer or Commissioner or Principal Commissioner or Chief Commissioner or Principal Chief Commissioner, as the case may be, may, in lieu of payment of the refund, set off the amount to be refunded or any part of that amount against the sum, if any, remaining payable under this Act by the person to whom the refund is due. The person must be given an intimation in writing of the proposed set-off, and after considering the reply, the Assessing Officer shall make an order of set-off. This provision is relevant for startups that may have refunds due but also have outstanding tax demands.

title: Penalty for Concealment of Income
content: If the Assessing Officer or Commissioner (Appeals) or Commissioner in the course of any proceedings under this Act is satisfied that any person has concealed the particulars of his income or furnished inaccurate particulars of such income, he may direct that such person shall pay by way of penalty a sum which shall not be less than one hundred percent but which shall not exceed three hundred percent of the amount of tax sought to be evaded by reason of the concealment of particulars of his income or the furnishing of inaccurate particulars. The penalty provision is a significant deterrent and startups must ensure accurate disclosure of all income in their returns.

title: Tax on Long-Term Capital Gains on Listed Securities
content: Notwithstanding anything contained in Section 112, where the total income of an assessee includes any income chargeable under the head Capital gains arising from the transfer of a long-term capital asset being an equity share in a company or a unit of an equity-oriented fund or a unit of a business trust, on which securities transaction tax has been paid, the tax payable by the assessee on such capital gains shall be calculated at the rate of ten percent on such capital gains exceeding one lakh rupees. The cost of acquisition for computing gains shall be the higher of the actual cost or the fair market value as on January 31, 2018, thereby grandfathering gains accrued up to that date. This provision is significant for startup founders and investors exiting through listed equity.
